consent.body: We use optional Google Analytics cookies to understand how the site is used. The site works fully without them, and we never sell your information. Would you like to allow analytics cookies?

disclaimer.title: Important Notice
disclaimer.text: This tool provides educational information to help you navigate medication assistance options. It is not a substitute for professional medical advice. Always consult your transplant team or healthcare provider with any questions about your medical condition or treatment.

header.title: Education & Resources
header.subtitle: Learn about programs that help pay for your medicines. Find out which ones work best for you.

totalAssist.title: TotalAssist Is Here, the Nation's Largest Charitable Patient Assistance Program
totalAssist.p1: The Patient Advocate Foundation and PAN Foundation have merged to launch TotalAssist, the nation's largest charitable patient assistance program. Over a combined 50 years, the two organizations have helped 3.8 million patients and granted over $7 billion in financial assistance.
totalAssist.p2: As of July 1, 2026, TotalAssist combines all financial assistance into one place, with nearly 150 disease-specific and health equity funds, no wait lists, and instant eligibility decisions in most cases. Grants help cover medication copays, coinsurance, deductibles, insurance premiums, office visit charges, and treatment-related costs. Eligibility generally extends to patients with income at or below 500% of the Federal Poverty Level, and a six-month lookback can reimburse recent expenses.

gotDenied.title: Got Denied?
gotDenied.text: Insurance said no? Don't worry, you can fight back. Learn why denials happen and how to appeal. We have letter templates to help your doctor write on your behalf.

emergency.title: Out of medication, or about to be?
emergency.intro: Anti-rejection medicine is not something you can safely skip. If cost or a refill problem means you are running low, act today. Here is how to get a bridge supply while you sort out the cost.
emergency.call911: If this is a medical emergency, call 911.
emergency.call911Text: Signs of rejection, fever, pain over the transplant, less urine, sudden weight gain, or feeling very unwell, need your transplant team right away. Do not wait. Never change or stop your doses on your own.
emergency.step1Title: Call your transplant team first, today
emergency.step1Text: This is the most important step. Your transplant center handles this all the time. Ask for the transplant pharmacist or social worker. They can often send an emergency prescription, give you samples, or start a fast assistance request the same day. Tell them plainly: "I am about to run out and I cannot afford the refill."
emergency.step2Title: Ask your pharmacy for an emergency supply
emergency.step2Pre: Many states let a pharmacist give a few days of a regular medicine in an emergency so you do not miss doses. Ask:
emergency.step2Quote: "Can you do an emergency fill while I sort out the cost or the refill?"
emergency.step2Post: Bring your pill bottle or the medicine name and dose.
emergency.step3Title: Ask the drug maker about a bridge supply
emergency.step3Text: Some manufacturers give a free short-term "bridge" or "quick start" supply while your Patient Assistance Program application is reviewed, so you are covered during the wait. Your transplant team can request this, or call the drug maker's patient support line.
emergency.step3Link: Find your medication and its manufacturer →
emergency.step4Text: Some copay foundations review urgent cases faster. It is worth a call when you are in a pinch. See the trusted groups in our directory.
emergency.readyTitle: Have this ready when you call
readyItems: Your insurance card (if you have one)
emergency.dontStopTitle: Do not just stop taking your medicine.
emergency.dontStopText: Missing anti-rejection doses can put your transplant at risk. If you truly cannot get a full supply, ask your transplant team whether a safe short-term plan is possible, but let them make that call, not you.
emergency.dontStopCta: Get help applying for longer-term assistance

oop.title: Mixing Programs
oop.intro: Most transplant patients use a mix of help programs to pay for their medicines. It's like a puzzle.

negotiation.title: Medicare Drug Price Negotiation
negotiation.lead: For the first time ever, Medicare talked drug makers into lower prices.
negotiation.p1: The first new prices start January 1, 2026, with big savings. Examples: Eliquis drops from $521 to $231/month (56% less), and Januvia drops from $527 to $113/month (79% less). This will save Medicare patients $1.5 billion in 2026 alone.
negotiation.p2: The first 10 drugs include medicines for heart failure (Entresto), diabetes (Jardiance, Farxiga, Januvia), blood clots (Eliquis, Xarelto), immune diseases (Stelara, Enbrel), blood cancers (Imbruvica), and insulin (NovoLog/Fiasp).
negotiation.p3: About 8.8 million Medicare Part D patients used these medicines in 2023. A second round of 15 drugs was selected for 2027 prices.
negotiation.round3P1: CMS selected 15 new drugs for negotiation, including the first-ever Part B drugs. New prices take effect January 1, 2028.
negotiation.round3P2: Transplant-relevant picks: Orencia (abatacept, related to Belatacept), Trulicity (dulaglutide, for post-transplant diabetes), Xolair (omalizumab, lung transplant), Anoro Ellipta (lung transplant). Also includes Botox, Biktarvy, Cimzia, Cosentyx, Entyvio, Erleada, Kisqali, Lenvima, Rexulti, Verzenio, and Xeljanz.

glp1.title: Medicare GLP-1 Coverage for Weight Loss
glp1.lead: A big change: Medicare will now cover weight loss drugs.
glp1.p1: Starting mid-2026, Medicare will cover certain GLP-1 drugs for weight loss, ending a rule that kept these drugs out of Part D.
glp1.p2: Covered drugs: Wegovy and Zepbound will be covered for patients with weight-related health issues. Your copay should be about $50/month after you meet your deductible.
glp1.p3: Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk agreed to give these drugs at lower prices for Medicare patients.

m3p.title: Medicare Prescription Payment Plan
m3p.lead: A big change from new laws
m3p.p1: Since 2025, Medicare Part D patients can spread out their drug costs over the year instead of paying large amounts all at once.
m3p.p2: This program makes your costs more steady and easier to manage.
m3p.p3: Ask your Part D plan about this payment plan during open enrollment.

pick.title: Part D vs Medicare Advantage: Picking Your Plan
pick.intro: Knowing the differences can save you a lot of money on transplant medicines.
pick.partDDesc: A drug plan that works with Original Medicare. It's a separate plan just for medicines.
pick.maDesc: An all-in-one plan that covers both doctor visits AND medicines.

compare.title: How to Compare Plans for Your Medicines
compare.step1Text: Use the Medicare Plan Finder tool at medicare.gov to see which plans cover your drugs and how much they cost.
compare.step1LinkAria: Visit Medicare Plan Finder (opens in new tab)
compare.step2Text: Look at monthly fees, deductibles, copays, and which pharmacies you can use. Remember the $2,100 yearly limit (2026) when you add up costs.
compare.step3Text: If you have low income, the Extra Help program can cut costs for Part D and MA-PD plans. It can remove your monthly fees and deductible ($615 in 2026) and cap copays for both generic and brand-name drugs.
compare.step3LinkAria: Apply for Extra Help (opens in new tab)
compare.step4Text: Call your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for free help picking a plan.
compare.step4LinkAria: Find Your Local SHIP (opens in new tab)

parts.title: Medicare Parts for Transplant Drugs
parts.intro: Knowing which part of Medicare pays for your transplant medicines is key.
parts.partADesc: Pays for drugs given while you're in the hospital.
parts.partBDesc: Pays for some drugs you get at clinics. Also covers anti-rejection drugs if you don't have Part D.
parts.partDDesc: Pays for most of the pills you take at home each day.

strategies.title: Strategies by Insurance Type
strategies.commercialItem1: Use manufacturer copay cards to lower what you pay at the pharmacy.
strategies.medicareItem2: Check Cash Prices for generics to compare savings.

oop.keyInsightText: Many patients mix programs, for example, a Medicare patient might get foundation grants to help with copays. Also check cash prices for generic drugs.

glossary.title: Insurance Words Made Simple
glossary.intro: Knowing what these words mean helps you handle costs and find help.
glossary.premiumDef: The amount you pay each month to have insurance. Think of it like a gym membership - you pay every month whether you use it or not.
glossary.deductibleDef: The amount you must pay yourself before your insurance starts helping. If your deductible is $500, you pay the first $500 of medicine costs. After that, insurance helps pay.
glossary.copayDef: A fixed amount you pay each time you get medicine. Example: You might pay $10 for each prescription, and insurance pays the rest.
glossary.coinsuranceDef: The percentage you pay after meeting your deductible. If you have 20% coinsurance, you pay $20 for every $100 of medicine cost. Insurance pays the other $80.
glossary.oopMaxDef: The most money you will pay in one year. Once you reach this amount, insurance pays 100% of covered medicines. This is your safety net.
glossary.formularyDef: The list of medicines your insurance will cover. Think of it as the insurance company's menu. If your medicine isn't on the list, you might pay more or need special approval.
glossary.tierDef: How insurance groups medicines by cost. Lower tiers (1-2) cost less. Higher tiers (3-4-5) cost more.
glossary.paDef: When you need insurance company approval before they will pay for a medicine. Your doctor must explain why you need that specific medicine.
glossary.paWiserPre: The WISeR (Wasteful and Inappropriate Service Reduction) model requires prior authorization for 17 outpatient Medicare Part B services in 6 states: Arizona, New Jersey, Ohio, Oklahoma, Texas, and Washington. While it does not directly affect transplant medications, it may add steps for some procedures.
glossary.paWiserLink: Learn more on our Appeals page
glossary.genericDef: A medicine that works the same as a brand name but costs less. Like store-brand cereal versus name-brand - same thing, different package.
glossary.brandDef: The original version of a medicine made by the company that invented it. Usually costs more than generic.
glossary.specialtyDef: Expensive medicines that need special handling or monitoring. Most transplant medicines are specialty medications. They often cost $600+ per month.
glossary.partDDef: The part of Medicare that helps pay for prescription medicines. You must sign up for this separately.
glossary.oopCapDef: A yearly limit on what you pay out of pocket for Medicare Part D drugs. In 2026 the cap is $2,100. Once you reach it, you pay $0 for covered drugs for the rest of the year.
glossary.catastrophicDef: After you reach the yearly Part D out-of-pocket cap ($2,100 in 2026), Medicare pays 100% and you pay $0 for covered drugs for the rest of the year.
glossary.inNetworkDef: A pharmacy that works with your insurance. You pay less here. Think of it as a preferred partner.
glossary.outNetworkDef: A pharmacy that doesn't have a deal with your insurance. You might pay more or get no coverage here.
glossary.pbmDef: The company that manages prescription drug coverage for your insurance. They decide which medicines are covered and how much they cost.
glossary.papDef: Free or low-cost medicine programs run by drug companies. If you can't afford your medicine, these programs might help.
glossary.copayCardDef: Help from drug companies to lower what you pay for brand name medicines. Important: These often don't work with Medicare.
glossary.mfrDef: Help directly from the company that makes your medicine. This can be free medicine, copay help, or discounts.
glossary.mailDef: A pharmacy that ships medicine to your home. Usually gives you 90 days of medicine at once. Often costs less than getting 30 days at a local pharmacy.
glossary.stepDef: When insurance requires you to try a cheaper medicine first before they'll pay for a more expensive one. Like having to try the basic version before getting the premium version.
glossary.exclusionDef: A medicine that insurance will not cover at all. You must pay the full price yourself or find other help.
glossary.whyTitle: Why This Matters for Transplant Patients
glossary.whyIntro: Your anti-rejection medicines are specialty medications. They are expensive. Understanding these words helps you:
whyItems: Find help when costs are too high
Talk to your transplant team about options
askItems: What tier are my transplant medicines?
When do I reach my yearly spending limit?
Are my medicines on the covered medicines list?
Can I use mail order to save money?

directory.title: Trusted Resource Directory
directory.emptyText: We're working on adding trusted resources. Check back soon for helpful links and organizations.

wiser.title: New in 2026: CMS WISeR Prior Authorization Pilot
wiser.intro: Starting January 2026, CMS launched the WISeR (Wasteful and Inappropriate Service Reduction) model. This adds prior authorization for 17 outpatient Medicare Part B services in 6 states.
wiser.know1: WISeR covers outpatient procedures (nerve stimulators, spinal procedures, wound care), not transplant medications
wiser.know2: Your immunosuppressant drugs are NOT affected
wiser.know3: If you need any of the 17 listed procedures (e.g., wound care with skin substitutes), your provider must get prior authorization
wiser.know4: Coverage decisions are made within 72 hours (48 hours for urgent cases)
wiser.footer: The WISeR model runs from January 2026 through December 2031. It applies only to Original Medicare (fee-for-service), not Medicare Advantage plans. If you are in one of these states and need a covered procedure, talk to your provider about the prior authorization process.

insurance.note: Each type of insurance has its own benefits, costs, and best ways to save.
insurance.infographicAlt: Comparison chart of transplant medication help by insurance type: Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid, and Uninsured
insurance.infographicDesc: This infographic compares what transplant medication help is available across four insurance types. Commercial Insurance: Insurance through your job or that you buy on your own. You may qualify for manufacturer copay cards, patient assistance programs, and copay foundations. You pay monthly premiums, copays, and deductibles, then insurance covers the rest. Common problems include high deductibles, prior authorization requirements, and copay accumulators. Next step: search your medications to find copay cards and assistance programs. Medicare: Government insurance for people 65 and older or with disabilities. You may qualify for patient assistance programs, Extra Help or Low Income Subsidy, and copay foundations. Part D covers most transplant drugs with monthly premiums and copays. Common problems include Part D costs and you cannot use manufacturer copay cards. Next step: check if you qualify for Extra Help and search for patient assistance programs. Medicaid: Government insurance for people with low income. You may qualify for patient assistance programs and state-specific programs. Covers most transplant drugs with very low or no copays. Common problems include limited pharmacy networks, formulary restrictions, and coverage ending if income changes. Next step: search your medications and check with your state Medicaid office. Uninsured or Self-Pay: No current insurance coverage. You may qualify for manufacturer patient assistance programs for free medications, state programs, and discount cards. You pay the full retail price without help. Common problems include very high costs, no negotiated rates, and difficulty affording specialty medications. Next step: apply for patient assistance programs immediately as most offer free medications for qualifying patients. The infographic concludes with an encouraging message: You are not alone and there is help available no matter what type of insurance you have.

medicareGuide.title: Medicare Guide
partCItems: Combines A, B, and usually D
medicareGuide.chooseTitle: Part D vs Medicare Advantage: Choosing Your Plan

commercial.title: Commercial Insurance
commercial.intro: Your main payer for the first 30 months after transplant.
keyPoints: Can use most drug maker PAPs
May need to use a certain pharmacy
commercial.strategy: Start with drug maker PAPs, then use foundations for any copays or deductibles left over.

va.title: VA Health Care
va.strategy: Priority Groups 1–6: $0 for medicines. Use the VA pharmacy first. Work with your VA transplant team.
va.linkAria: Visit VA Prescription Information (opens in new tab)

tricare.strategy: Use the military pharmacy ($0 copay). Use TRICARE mail order for ongoing medicines. Check the drug list search tool.

noInsurance.title: No Insurance
items: Start with drug maker PAPs
Check Cost Plus Drugs prices
Use discount tools (GoodRx, SingleCare, etc.)
Look into Medicaid or Marketplace plans

medicaid.title: Medicaid (State-Based)
medicaid.intro: A program for people with low income. What it covers depends on your state.
medicaid.goAria: Visit your state's Medicaid website (opens in new tab)
medicaid.leaving: You are leaving this app to visit a government website.

ihs.title: Indian Health Service / Tribal Programs
ihs.intro: For American Indian and Alaska Native patients who qualify.
ihs.findText: Use the official IHS locator to find clinics and pharmacies near you.
ihs.findAria: Find an IHS facility (opens in new tab)
ihs.strategy: Use your local IHS or Urban Indian Program, usually $0 cost.

deductibleTrap.title: The Deductible Trap
deductibleTrap.subtitle: Why Prescription Discount Cards Can Cost Transplant Patients More

warning.title: ⚠️ Important Warning for Transplant Patients
warning.lead: Using discount cards like GoodRx or SingleCare can cost you THOUSANDS of extra dollars each year.
warning.boxTitle: Watch out for the "deductible trap." Here's what happens:
warning.boxP1Pre: If you use a discount card,
warning.boxP1Highlight: the money you pay does NOT count toward your deductible
warning.boxP2: You still have to pay your full deductible later. This can cost you more money over the year.
warning.boxP3: What to do: Ask your insurance company when to start using discount cards.

example.title: Real-World Example
example.trapM112: Pay discounted price (~$200/month) but NONE counts toward yearly limit
example.trapResult: You NEVER reach your yearly limit, so you pay all year long
example.takeaway: Small savings today = thousands of dollars in extra costs over the year.

whenToUse.title: When Should Transplant Patients Use Discount Cards?
whenToUse.s1Title: Scenario 1: Drug NOT Covered by Insurance
whenToUse.s1Text: For a medicine NOT on your insurance's list and no cheaper generic option is available.
whenToUse.s2Text: For short-term medicines not related to your transplant (like a round of antibiotics) where the discount price is less than your copay.
whenToUse.s3Text: If you have a very high deductible AND the cash price is much lower, but even then, think hard about the cost of not reaching your yearly limit.
whenToUse.neverP1: Your daily anti-rejection medicines like Tacrolimus, Mycophenolate, or Cyclosporine.
whenToUse.neverP2: These are the medicines that will help you reach your yearly limit fast.

alternatives.title: Better Alternatives
alternatives.papsText: Free or low-cost medicines straight from drug makers.
alternatives.foundationsText: Groups that help pay for your copays and deductibles.
alternatives.copayText: Help from drug makers for brand-name medicines (not for Medicare patients).

takeaways.title: Key Takeaways
takeaways.t1Bold: Your goal every year is to hit your yearly spending limit (the most you pay each year).
takeaways.t1Rest: Once you reach it, insurance pays 100% for the rest of the year.
takeaways.t2Bold: Discount cards stop your payments from counting toward this goal.
takeaways.t2Rest: They may save you a few dollars per medicine, but cost you thousands over the year.
takeaways.t3Bold: Always use PAPs and co-pay foundations first.
takeaways.t3Rest: These programs help you pay for medicines AND help you reach your yearly limit faster.
takeaways.t4Rest: They can connect you with people who know these programs and can help you through the system.

deductibleTrap.questionsText: Not sure if a discount card is right for you? Ask your transplant center's money helper or social worker. They can look at your insurance plan and help you choose.

diversion.title: When Insurance Sends You Elsewhere
diversion.subtitle: Some plans try to avoid paying for your drugs. Learn how to spot this and what to do.

warning.title: Warning: Your Plan May Send You Away
warning.lead: Some plans don't want to pay for costly drugs. They may make you get free drugs from drug makers first.
warning.boxP1Highlight: "We won't pay for your drug until you ask a drug maker for free medicine first."
warning.boxP2: This saves money for your plan. But it can leave you without medicine while you wait. And you might not get the free drugs.

types.title: Three Ways Plans Do This
types.intro: These tricks have fancy names. But they all do the same thing: make someone else pay for your drugs.

acc.title: Copay Accumulator
acc.pre: Drug makers give you copay help. Your plan takes that help. But
acc.highlight: it does not count toward what you owe for the year
why: Your copay card runs out faster
When it runs out, you owe the full price
You keep paying all year long

max.title: Copay Maximizer
max.pre: Your plan spreads your copay card over the year. But
max.highlight: the plan keeps the savings, not you
why: The drug maker's help goes to your plan, not you
You still pay more than you should
When the help runs out, you owe a lot

afp.title: Alternative Funding (AFP)
afp.highlight: "We won't cover this drug. Go get it free from the drug maker."
why: You wait weeks or months for your drugs
You might not get the free drugs
If you don't get free drugs, you pay full price
Your plan saves money. You take the risk.

identify.title: How to Tell If This Is Happening to You
signs: Copay card money is not showing on your plan tracker
They tell you to call another company first
You must ask for free drugs before your plan will help
questions: "Will my copay card count toward what I owe?"
"Do I have to ask for free drugs first?"

protect.title: How to Protect Yourself
protect.s1Text: Look for words like "accumulator," "maximizer," or "alternative funding." These are clues.
protect.s2Title: Keep Track of What You Pay
protect.s2Text: Save your receipts. Check if your copay card money shows up on your plan's tracker. If not, you may have an accumulator plan.
protect.s3Text: If your plan won't cover your drug, you can fight back. Ask your transplant team to write a letter saying you need this drug.
protect.s4Text: Many states have laws to protect you. Call your state insurance office. They can tell you your rights and help you file a complaint.
protect.s5Title: Talk to Your Boss (If You Get Insurance From Work)
protect.s5Text: Your boss picks the plan. Tell HR how this hurts you. Ask them to pick a better plan.

stateLaws.title: States That Protect You
stateLaws.intro: Some states made laws to stop these tricks. In these states, copay card money must count toward what you owe.
stateLaws.listTitle: States With Laws to Help (as of 2026):
stateLaws.list: Arizona, Arkansas, California, Connecticut, Delaware, Georgia, Illinois, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Maryland, Minnesota, Missouri, New Jersey, New York, North Carolina, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Puerto Rico, Tennessee, Texas, Virginia, Washington, West Virginia, and more.
stateLaws.note: Call your state insurance office to learn about new laws. More states add protections every year.

whyMatters.title: Why Transplant Patients Must Know This
whyMatters.t1Rest: Missing doses can hurt your transplant. Waiting weeks for free drugs is risky.
whyMatters.t2Bold: Free drugs are not a sure thing.
whyMatters.t2Rest: Drug makers have rules. You might not qualify. The money might run out.
whyMatters.t3Rest: You pay every month. But some plans make drug makers pay for your drugs instead.
whyMatters.t4Rest: When your copay card runs out, you may owe a lot of money all at once.

help.title: Need Help?
help.text: Think this is happening to you? Talk to your transplant center's money helper or social worker. They know how to help.

mental.title: Mental Health Resources
mental.subtitle: Your mental health matters. Get free, private support when you need it.

crisis.title: Need to Talk to Someone Right Now?
crisis.name: National Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
crisis.callText: Dial or text 988 from any phone

samhsa.title: SAMHSA
samhsa.desc: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services - Find treatment centers, support groups, and mental health help near you.

transplant.title: Transplant & Mental Health
transplant.intro: It's normal to feel worried, sad, or stressed during your transplant journey. You're not alone.
items: Ask your transplant team about counseling services
Many transplant centers have social workers and mental health experts
Medicare and most insurance plans cover mental health services

nami.aria: Visit NAMI Support Groups (opens in new tab)
nami.title: NAMI Support Groups
nami.desc: NAMI offers free support groups for people with mental health issues and their families.

findTreatment.desc: A government site to help you learn about mental health, find treatment, and find services near you.

caregivers.title: Support for Caregivers & Care Partners
caregivers.intro: You are essential to the transplant journey. Your well-being matters too. Caring for a loved one can feel rewarding but also physically and emotionally demanding. Take care of yourself so you can keep providing the best support.
tips: Set boundaries and ask for help when you need it
Take breaks and maintain your own health appointments
Connect with other caregivers who understand your experience
Consider counseling or support groups for caregivers
caregivers.nacAria: Visit National Alliance for Caregiving (opens in new tab)
caregivers.nacDesc: A national nonprofit coalition dedicated to advancing family caregiving through research, innovation, and advocacy. Find resources, research, and support for your caregiving journey.

remember.title: Remember
remember.text: Seeking help for mental health is a sign of strength, not weakness. The transplant journey is physically and emotionally demanding. Taking care of your mental health is just as important as taking your medications. If you're struggling, reach out, there are people who want to help.

simulator.title: Insurance Change Simulator
simulator.subtitle: See how your medication options change when switching insurance
simulator.comingSoon: Detailed transition info for this combination coming soon. Contact your transplant coordinator for guidance.
simulator.selectDifferent: Please select different insurance types to see the transition changes.

commercial_medicare.title: Commercial → Medicare
commercial_medicare.timing: Common at 65 or after 24 months on SSDI

changes.title: Copay Cards
changes.desc: Most manufacturer copay cards are NOT allowed with Medicare. This is a major change.

changes.title: PAP Access
changes.desc: You may now qualify for Patient Assistance Programs if your income is low enough (many have higher limits for Medicare patients).

changes.title: Foundation Help
changes.desc: Foundations like PAN, PANF, and HealthWell CAN help Medicare patients with copays.

changes.title: Part B-ID Option
changes.desc: Kidney transplant patients may qualify for Part B coverage of immunosuppressants (Part B-ID). Premium: $121.60/month in 2026 (up from $110.40 in 2025), plus 20% coinsurance after $283 deductible.

changes.title: 2026 Cap
changes.desc: Annual out-of-pocket cap on Part D drugs: $2,100 in 2026 (up from $2,000 in 2025).

commercial_medicare.action: Apply to manufacturer PAPs 3 months before your Medicare start date. Register with foundations early as funds run out.

commercial_medicaid.title: Commercial → Medicaid
commercial_medicaid.timing: Due to income change or disability

changes.title: Copay Cards
changes.desc: Copay cards typically cannot be used with Medicaid.

changes.title: Low/No Cost
changes.desc: Medicaid typically has $0-$3 copays for prescriptions.

changes.title: PAP Access
changes.desc: Some PAPs still available as secondary assistance.

changes.title: Covered Medicines
changes.desc: May need to switch to Medicaid-preferred medications or get prior authorization.

commercial_medicaid.action: Work with your transplant coordinator to ensure your medications are on the Medicaid covered medicines list before switching.

medicaid_medicare.title: Medicaid → Medicare (Dual Eligible)
medicaid_medicare.timing: At age 65 or qualifying for Medicare

changes.title: Dual Eligible Status
changes.desc: You may qualify as "dual eligible" and keep some Medicaid benefits alongside Medicare.

changes.title: Extra Help/LIS
changes.desc: Likely qualify for Low Income Subsidy (Extra Help) which covers most Part D costs.

changes.title: Primary Payer
changes.desc: Medicare becomes primary, Medicaid secondary for covered services.

changes.title: MSP Programs
changes.desc: Medicare Savings Programs can pay your Part B premium ($202.90/month in 2026).

medicaid_medicare.action: Contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for free counseling on dual eligible benefits.

commercial_marketplace.title: Commercial → ACA Marketplace
commercial_marketplace.timing: Job loss, self-employment, or retirement before 65

changes.title: Copay Cards OK
changes.desc: Manufacturer copay cards CAN be used with Marketplace plans.

changes.title: Subsidies Available
changes.desc: Premium tax credits based on income can significantly reduce costs.

changes.title: Network Changes
changes.desc: Check if your transplant center and pharmacy are in-network before enrolling.

changes.title: CSR Plans
changes.desc: If income <250% FPL, Silver plans offer reduced deductibles and copays.

commercial_marketplace.action: Use healthcare.gov to compare plans. Filter by your medications and doctors to find the best fit.

uninsured_medicare.title: Uninsured → Medicare

changes.title: Drug Coverage
changes.desc: Part D provides prescription coverage with $2,100 out-of-pocket cap (2026).

changes.title: PAPs + Foundations
changes.desc: Full access to Patient Assistance Programs and foundation copay help.

changes.title: Discount Cards
changes.desc: GoodRx/SingleCare typically not usable once you have Part D coverage.

changes.title: Part B-ID
changes.desc: Kidney patients: Part B-ID covers immunosuppressants even without Part A/B. Premium: $121.60/month (2026).

uninsured_medicare.action: Enroll during your Initial Enrollment Period. Apply for Extra Help (LIS) if income-limited.

uninsured_medicaid.title: Uninsured → Medicaid

changes.title: Coverage
changes.desc: Comprehensive coverage including prescriptions, often $0-$3 copays.

changes.title: Retroactive
changes.desc: Coverage goes back up to 3 months before you apply.

changes.title: Covered Medicines
changes.desc: Must use Medicaid covered medicines list - may need prior approval for some transplant drugs.

uninsured_medicaid.action: Apply at your state Medicaid office or healthcare.gov. Bring proof of income and transplant papers.

uninsured_marketplace.title: Uninsured → ACA Marketplace
uninsured_marketplace.timing: Open enrollment or qualifying life event

changes.title: Subsidies
changes.desc: Help paying for insurance based on income - may get $0 premium plans.

changes.title: Copay Cards

selfpay.name: Paying full cost yourself, with no insurance.
selfpay.tag: Between jobs, pre-Medicare, or otherwise without coverage.

People waiting for Medicare or Medicaid to start

selfpay.warn: Catastrophic out-of-pocket costs. Risk of medical debt. Skipped doses if cost feels impossible.
steps: Apply for manufacturer Patient Assistance Programs first.
Negotiate cash prices and ask about payment plans.
Re-check Medicaid and Marketplace plan eligibility.

generics.title: Generics vs. Brand-Name Medicines
generics.intro: Generics can save you a lot of money, and for most medicines they are a smart, safe choice. For transplant patients, there is just one important rule that keeps them safe. Here is what to know.

real.title: Generics are real medicine
real.li1: • A generic has the same active ingredient as the brand name.
real.li2: • The FDA approves it and requires it to work the same way.
real.li3: • It usually costs a lot less.
real.li4: • For most medicines, choosing the generic is a safe way to save money.

extraCare.title: Transplant drugs need extra care
extraCare.p1: Anti-rejection drugs like tacrolimus (Prograf), cyclosporine, sirolimus (Rapamune), and everolimus are "narrow therapeutic index" drugs. That means even a small change in how much reaches your blood matters:
extraCare.li1: • Too little → your body could reject the organ.
extraCare.li2: • Too much → more side effects and toxicity.
extraCare.p2: Different makers can be absorbed a little differently. So the real risk is not the generic itself. The risk is switching (brand to generic, or one generic maker to another).

safeWay.title: The safe way to save with a generic
safeWay.li1: Pick one version and stay on it. A generic is fine. Just keep the same manufacturer every time you refill.
safeWay.li2: Ask your pharmacy to always give you the same maker, and to call you before any switch.
safeWay.li3: Ask your doctor to write the maker on your prescription (or "Do Not Substitute" if your team wants brand-name).
safeWay.li4: After any change, ask for a blood test within a week.
safeWay.li5: Always tell your transplant team before you change anything.

brand.title: When to ask for brand-name
brand.intro: You may want to ask for brand-name if:
brand.li1: • Your drug levels have been steady on it.
brand.li2: • You had problems after a past switch.
brand.appealLink: If insurance says no, learn how to appeal

bottomLine.title: The bottom line
bottomLine.text: Generics can save you money and be safe. The key is staying consistent: use the same version every time, and keep your transplant team in the loop. Never switch on your own just to save money.

generics.footer: Have a question about a switch? Call your transplant team before your next refill.

whatArePaps.title: What are Patient Assistance Programs?
whatArePaps.p1: Patient Assistance Programs (PAPs) are programs created by drug companies and medical supply makers to help patients access affordable medications. These programs provide prescription medications at no cost or low cost for individuals who need help affording their medications.
whatArePaps.p2: Important: Patient Assistance Programs are for everyone who needs affordable medications - not just those without insurance. Many insured patients use these programs to reduce their medication costs.

pap.title: Patient Assistance Programs
pap.text: Programs for everyone who needs affordable medications. Get prescription medications at no cost or minimal fee, regardless of insurance status.

copay.title: Copay Cards
copay.text: Manufacturer copay cards help reduce your out-of-pocket costs at the pharmacy.
copay.eligibility: Who can use copay cards: Only for insurance you get through work or buy on your own. Not for Medicare, Medicaid, or VA benefits. If you have these, look at Patient Assistance Programs instead.
copay.imgAlt: How to find and apply for copay cards
copay.imgDesc: This infographic explains how to find and apply for medication copay cards. The process has 3 main steps. First, visit the medication manufacturer's website or use our Copay Card Finder tool. Second, check eligibility requirements - most copay cards are available if you have commercial insurance and your medication costs more than $50 per month. Third, complete the online application which takes 5-10 minutes and requires your prescription information and insurance details. Most copay cards are approved instantly and can be used at your pharmacy the same day. Copay cards can reduce your out-of-pocket cost from hundreds of dollars to as little as $0-$25 per month.

hero.title: The Evidence: Why Medication & Financial Navigation Matters
hero.intro: New peer-reviewed research confirms what transplant patients have always known — the cost of survival is a clinical problem, not just a personal one. Two national studies now document it across the entire transplant journey: before transplant, financial burden threatens candidacy; after transplant, medication costs threaten the graft itself.

burden.title: 1 in 4 Liver Transplant Candidates Faces High Financial Burden
burden.intro: A 2026 multicenter U.S. study published in Hepatology Communications — spanning more than a dozen transplant centers including Massachusetts General Hospital, University of Minnesota, USC, Duke, and Montefiore Einstein — surveyed 453 adults undergoing liver transplant evaluation. The findings were stark:
burden.stat1: reported high financial burden — out-of-pocket medical costs of 10% or more of household income
burden.stat2: were still employed at the time of transplant evaluation
burden.stat3: of high-burden patients delayed or went without medical care (vs. 33.5% of low-burden patients)
burden.stat4: could not pay for basic necessities like food, heat, or rent (vs. 11.3%)

odds.title: Financial Burden Predicts Distress Across Every Dimension
odds.intro: Financial burden is not a side issue. After adjusting for age, income, insurance, disease severity, and other factors, patients with high financial burden had dramatically higher odds of harm:
odds.caption: Adjusted odds of financial distress outcomes for high-burden vs. low-burden liver transplant candidates
odds.row3: Unable to pay for basic necessities
odds.row6: Any behavioral financial distress (delaying or skipping care)
odds.work: Financial burden also reached the workplace: employed patients with high financial burden missed twice as much work as their low-burden peers (34.1% vs. 16.9% absenteeism). For transplant candidates, employment is not incidental — it is often the source of the insurance and income that candidacy depends on.

graft.title: After Transplant, the Burden Doesn't End — It Threatens the Graft
graft.intro: The largest patient survey ever commissioned by the American Society of Transplantation, published in the American Journal of Transplantation in 2025, gathered 10,091 responses from transplant recipients across 232 U.S. transplant centers — spanning every organ, all ages, and every insurance type, at an average of 6.6 years post-transplant. Its cost findings should alarm every transplant program:
graft.stat1Label: of recipients missed a medication fill or refill in the past year because of cost
graft.stat2Label: skipped or reduced immunosuppressant doses due to cost "sometimes" or "often"
graft.p1: For transplant recipients, these are not ordinary adherence lapses. Missed immunosuppressant doses are among the strongest known drivers of rejection, graft loss, and return to the waitlist — outcomes that carry enormous human and financial cost for patients, programs, and payers alike.
graft.p2: Read together, the two studies describe a single continuum: financial burden that surfaces during transplant evaluation does not resolve after surgery — it converts into cost-related nonadherence that puts the transplanted organ at risk, years down the road.

gap.title: What the Researchers Recommend — and Where the System Falls Short
gap.quote: The study's authors conclude that routine screening for financial burden is critical in liver transplant candidates and should be paired with targeted strategies to mitigate its impact.
gap.text: That second half is the gap. Screening tells a transplant program which patients are drowning. It does not throw them a rope. A patient identified as high-burden still faces a fragmented maze of manufacturer programs, foundations, insurance rules, and paperwork — the same maze that caused 66% of high-burden patients to delay or forgo care in the first place.

tmn.title: Transplant Medication Navigator Is the Targeted Strategy
tmn.intro: TMN was built to be the intervention that the research calls for — the step between identifying financial burden and resolving it.
tmn.s1Text: TMN is a SMART on FHIR application listed on the Epic Connection Hub, with 500+ organizational downloads, so navigation can live inside the transplant center's own workflow.
tmn.s2Text: The right program depends on coverage. TMN routes commercially insured patients toward pharmaceutical copay cards and Medicare patients toward patient assistance programs (PAPs) and charitable foundations — two distinct pathways that patients routinely confuse, at real cost.
tmn.s3Text: Because the research shows the burden extends to premiums, bills, travel, and basic necessities, TMN also connects patients to copay foundations, case-management services, and low-cost pharmacy options such as Cost Plus Drugs.
tmn.s4Text: Created by a liver transplant recipient and national patient advocate — designed around how patients actually encounter these barriers, not how the system assumes they do.

whyNow.title: Why This Matters to Transplant Programs Now
whyNow.p1: Value-based accountability is arriving in transplantation. CMS's IOTA model now ties kidney transplant program payment to transplant volume and post-transplant outcomes — and the direction of travel is clear across all organs. This research shows that financial burden drives delayed care, missed work, and psychological distress: precisely the forces that erode candidacy, adherence, and graft survival.
whyNow.p2: Programs that screen for financial burden without an operational answer are documenting risk, not managing it.
whyNow.scopeText: the financial burden study examined liver transplant candidates specifically, so its exact figures should not be assumed identical for other organs. The AST survey, however, spanned all solid organ types — and its finding that cost drives missed fills and skipped doses in roughly 4 in 10 recipients confirms the problem is transplant-wide, not organ-specific.

crisis.notAlone: You are not alone. Help is available. Please follow these steps in order.
crisis.papText: Most manufacturers have a "Patient Assistance Program". This is your best route for free medication.
crisis.medicaidText: If you have low income, check if you qualify for state Medicaid or "Extra Help" (if on Medicare).
crisis.ihsText: You likely have coverage for these medications at $0 cost at IHS facilities.
crisis.notQualifyTitle: If you don’t qualify — or a fund is closed
crisis.notQualifyIntro: A “no” from one program is not the end. Try these next:
crisis.tryPrograms: Apply to several programs at once. Income limits and open funds change often, so don’t stop at one.
crisis.tryCharity: Ask your transplant center about charity care. Many centers have their own financial-assistance or hospital discount programs — your social worker can start one.
crisis.tryCash: Ask the pharmacy about cash and sliding-scale prices. Cost Plus Drugs and pharmacy discount programs are sometimes cheaper than a copay.

meta.title: Got Denied? Appeal Help for Transplant Patients | TransplantMedicationNavigator
meta.description: Learn how to appeal insurance denials for transplant medications. Includes step therapy exceptions, generic vs brand guidance, and medical necessity letter templates.

header.title: Got Denied? Don't Give Up.
header.tagline: You can fight back, and WIN!
header.intro: Insurance says "no" to almost everyone at some point. The good news? Many people win when they appeal. This interactive guide shows you how, step by step.
header.guideSubtitle: Print-ready guide with checklists & sample letters

whyDenied.title: Why Insurance Says No
whyDenied.intro: Your denial letter tells you why they said no. Here are the most common reasons:

priorAuth.title: Prior Auth Needed
priorAuth.description: Your doctor needs to ask permission first before the insurance will cover it.

notOnList.title: Not on the List
notOnList.description: Your drug isn't on their approved list (called a formulary).

stepTherapy.title: Try Something Else First
stepTherapy.description: They want you to try a cheaper drug first (step therapy).

quantityLimits.title: Quantity Limits
quantityLimits.description: They say you're getting too much of this drug.

generic.title: Use the Generic
generic.description: They want you to use a cheaper generic version.

whyDenied.remember: Remember: A denial is NOT the final answer. It's just the first "no", and you can fight it!

stepTherapy.title: Step Therapy: "Try This First"
stepTherapy.intro: Step therapy means your insurance wants you to try a cheaper drug before they'll pay for the one your doctor picked. Some people call this "fail first."

risky.title: Why This Can Be Risky
items: Anti-rejection drugs need exact blood levels. Small changes matter a lot.
Trying the wrong drug could hurt your transplant.
Your transplant team picked your drug for good reasons.
Switching means more blood tests and doctor visits.

canDo.title: What You Can Do
items: You can ask them to skip step therapy.
Transplant drugs often get approved when your doctor explains why.
A letter from your transplant doctor helps a lot.
Many states have laws that protect patients.

stepTherapy.proTip: Pro Tip: Ask your doctor about "step therapy exception", they can request this on your behalf!

basics.title: The Basics
items: Generic drugs have the same main ingredient as brand-name drugs.
The FDA says they work the same way.
For most drugs, generics are fine.

different.title: Why Transplant Drugs Are Different
different.intro: Anti-rejection drugs are special. Even tiny changes can cause problems:
different.tooLittle: Too little: Your body might reject the transplant

brandNeed.title: When You Might Need Brand Name
items: Your drug levels have been steady on this brand
You had problems when you switched before
Your transplant center requires brand-name
Your body has trouble absorbing the drug

howToAppeal.title: How to Appeal: Step by Step

tracker.instruction: Click each step when you've completed it to track your progress!

steps.title: Get It in Writing
steps.description: Ask for a letter that says exactly why they said no. You need this to know what to say in your appeal.
steps.tips: Call the number on your insurance card and ask for a 'written denial letter' or 'explanation of benefits.'

steps.title: Watch the Clock
steps.description: You usually have 30 to 60 days to appeal. Mark the date on your calendar. Don't miss it.
steps.tips: Set a phone reminder 2 weeks before your deadline!

steps.title: Get Your Papers Together
steps.description: Ask your transplant team for: a letter from your doctor, your transplant records, recent lab results, and notes about why other drugs won't work.
steps.tips: Use the letter builder below to help your doctor write a support letter!

steps.title: Send Your Appeal
steps.description: Send everything together. Keep copies of everything you send.
steps.tips: Send by fax or certified mail so you have proof it was received.

steps.title: Ask for a Fast Review If You Need It
steps.description: If you're going to run out of your medicine soon, ask for an expedited (fast) review. For transplant drugs, this almost always counts as urgent.
steps.tips: Say 'I need an expedited appeal because running out of my transplant medication is life-threatening.'

steps.title: Know What Comes Next
steps.description: If they say no again, you can ask for an external review by someone outside the company. Your state insurance office can also help.
steps.tips: Don't give up after the first appeal denial, external reviews often overturn decisions!

tracker.congratsTitle: Congratulations! You've completed all the steps!
tracker.congratsText: You're well on your way to winning your appeal. Stay strong!

letter.title: Write a Letter to Your Doctor
letter.subtitle: Ask your doctor to help with your appeal
letter.why: Why write to your doctor? Your doctor has the medical knowledge to explain why you need your specific medication. A letter of medical necessity from your doctor is often the key to winning an appeal.
letter.note: Note: Your doctor already has access to a detailed medical necessity letter template. This letter is for you to request their help and provide them with the information they need.
letter.denialReasonPlaceholder: e.g., Not on formulary, Step therapy required
letter.additionalInfoPlaceholder: Any other details that might help your doctor (e.g., previous drug reactions, why you need this specific medication, etc.)
letter.nextStep: Next step: Print this letter or email it to your doctor along with a copy of your denial letter.

medicare.title: Medicare Appeals
medicare.step1: Coverage Decision: Ask your plan to pay for your choice of pharmacy.
medicare.step2: Level 1 (Second Look): File within 65 days if they say no.
medicare.step3: Level 2 (Outside Review): An outside group looks at your case if denied again.

medicaid.title: Medicaid Appeals
medicaid.text: Each state runs its own appeals. Call your state's Medicaid office.

private.title: Private Insurance
private.text: You can ask your company to look again. If they still say no, you can ask for an outside review.

footer.title: Remember: You Are Not Alone
footer.text: Thousands of transplant patients successfully appeal their denials every year. Your health matters, and you have every right to fight for the medications you need. Stay strong and don't give up!

safety.title: Important Safety Note for Transplant Patients
safety.text: Never switch from brand name to generic (or between different generic manufacturers) without your transplant team's approval. In transplant medicine, slight variations in bioavailability can cause organ rejection. "Cheaper" isn't always "safe" if the manufacturer changes.

detail.heading: How to Afford {{name}}
detail.introTail: . Here are the ways to lower what you pay, from copay cards to programs that provide it for free.
detail.estPricePost: (varies by pharmacy; your price may be lower with insurance or assistance).
detail.copayText: For commercial or employer insurance. Copay cards can bring your cost down to as little as $0–$10 a month. Not valid with Medicare or Medicaid.
detail.papText: If you are uninsured, underinsured, or on Medicare/Medicaid and meet income limits, you may get {{name}} at no cost.
detail.papAsk: Ask your transplant team about the manufacturer or foundation options for this medication.
detail.grantsText: Charitable foundations can help cover copays and other costs, based on income and diagnosis.
detail.genericText: A generic {{generic}} is available and usually costs far less. For anti-rejection drugs, stay on the same manufacturer and talk to your transplant team before switching.
detail.genericLink: Generics vs. brand-name: what to know
detail.discountText: Compare GoodRx, SingleCare, Amazon Pharmacy, and Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drugs. For some medications the cash price beats your insurance copay.
detail.ctaTitle: Find out what you could pay for {{name}}
detail.ctaText: Answer a few questions and we'll match you to the copay cards, assistance programs, and prices you qualify for.

faq.copayQ: Is there a copay card for {{name}}?
faq.copayA: Yes. If you have commercial or employer insurance, the manufacturer copay card for {{name}} can lower your monthly cost, often to as little as $0–$10. Copay cards cannot be used with Medicare, Medicaid, or other government insurance.
faq.freeQ: How can I get {{name}} for free?
faq.freeA: If you are uninsured, underinsured, or on Medicare/Medicaid and meet income limits, a Patient Assistance Program (PAP) may provide {{name}} at no cost. Foundation grants can also cover your share of the cost. Take the free 2-minute quiz to see which programs you qualify for.
faq.genericA: Yes, a generic version of {{generic}} is available and usually costs much less. For transplant anti-rejection drugs, stay on the same manufacturer and talk to your transplant team before switching, because small changes can affect your drug levels.
faq.costA: Cash prices vary by pharmacy, but an estimated discount price is around {{price}}. Your actual cost depends on your insurance, and copay cards or assistance programs can reduce it further.

detail.disclaimer: Educational information only, not medical advice. Prices are estimates and vary by pharmacy and plan. Always talk to your transplant team before changing how or where you fill your medications.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro1: TransplantNav LLC ("we," "us," or "our") operates the Transplant Medication Navigator website (the "Website"). This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, disclose, and safeguard your information when you visit our Website.
privacy.intro2: Please read this Privacy Policy carefully. By using the Website, you agree to the collection and use of information in accordance with this policy.
privacy.s1Intro: We designed the Website so that information about your health stays with you. Here is exactly what is collected and where it goes:
privacy.cardDeviceTitle: Information that stays on your device
privacy.cardDeviceText: Your My Path Quiz answers, saved medication lists, savings entries, and progress are stored only in your own browser (local storage). They are not sent to our servers. We do not require an account, and we do not collect your name, email address, or payment information.
privacy.cardUsageText: We count events like page views, quiz starts and completions, medication searches, and clicks on assistance-program links, along with anonymous answer counts (for example, how many visitors selected each insurance type). These counts contain no name, email, IP address, or other identifier, and our collection endpoint actively rejects any request containing identifying fields.
privacy.cardFeedbackText: If you answer our feedback questions, surveys, or submit a community price report (medication, price paid, and pharmacy location), your responses are stored anonymously. We do not collect names, dates of birth, medical record numbers, or contact information with them, and we ask you not to include identifying details in free-text comments.
privacy.cardEpicText: If you choose to connect your health system account, your health system sends your active medication list through our secure server directly to your browser. We do not store your medication list, your record identifiers, or your access credentials on our servers. We record only that a login was completed at a given health system, with no information about who logged in.
privacy.cardChatText: If you use the chat assistant, the messages you type and the quiz answers you have provided (such as organ type, insurance type, and medication names) are processed by our artificial-intelligence provider, Anthropic, to generate a response. We do not store chat conversations in our database. Please do not include your name or contact information in chat messages.
privacy.cardCookiesText: Google Analytics runs only if you accept it in our consent banner. If you decline, or if your browser sends a Global Privacy Control signal, no Google Analytics script loads and no analytics cookies are set. You can change your choice anytime using the "Privacy Choices" link in the footer.
privacy.s2Intro: We use the information we collect in the following ways:
s2Items: To provide, operate, and maintain the Website
To improve the Website and understand which resources help patients most
To share aggregate, anonymous statistics (never individual data) with transplant centers and partners who use the tool
privacy.s3Intro: We may share your information in the following situations:
privacy.s3ProvidersText: The Website runs on the following providers, who process data on our behalf: Netlify (website hosting and serverless functions), Neon (database for anonymous usage data, feedback, and price reports), Anthropic (AI processing for the chat assistant), and Google Analytics (only if you accept analytics cookies). If you connect a health record, your own health system (for example, via Epic MyChart) is also involved in that exchange.
privacy.s3LegalText: We may disclose information if required by law or in response to valid requests by public authorities.
privacy.s3TransfersText: If we are involved in a merger, acquisition, or sale of assets, information may be transferred as part of that transaction.
privacy.s3NoSell: We do not sell your personal information, and we do not share it for advertising.
privacy.s4P1: The only tracking cookies we use are the optional Google Analytics cookies described above, and they are off until you accept them in the consent banner. We honor the Global Privacy Control browser signal as a decline. The Website also uses your browser's local storage to save your own quiz progress and medication lists on your device; that information never leaves your browser and you can clear it at any time through your browser settings. You can change your analytics choice anytime via the "Privacy Choices" link in the footer.
privacy.s5P1: We use administrative, technical, and physical security measures to protect your personal information. While we have taken reasonable steps to secure the information you provide to us, please be aware that no security measures are perfect or impenetrable, and we cannot guarantee that your information will not be accessed, disclosed, altered, or destroyed.
privacy.s6P1: Anonymous usage events, feedback responses, and community price reports are retained for up to 24 months, after which they are deleted or kept only as aggregate totals. Chat conversations are not stored in our systems. Information saved in your browser's local storage stays on your device until you clear it. We may retain information longer where necessary to comply with legal obligations or resolve disputes.
privacy.s7Intro: Depending on where you live, state privacy laws (such as the California Consumer Privacy Act or Washington's My Health My Data Act) may give you rights over your personal information and consumer health data, including:
privacy.s7AccessText: You may request access to the personal information we hold about you.
privacy.s7CorrectionText: You may request that we correct inaccurate or incomplete information.
privacy.s7DeletionText: You may request that we delete your personal information, subject to certain exceptions.
privacy.s7OptOutText: Decline or withdraw analytics consent anytime via the "Privacy Choices" link in the footer, or by using a browser that sends the Global Privacy Control signal.
privacy.s7P2: Because we do not store your name, contact information, or account data, in most cases we hold nothing that can be linked to you. To exercise any of these rights or ask what we hold, contact us using the information below and we will respond within the time required by applicable law. You may also authorize an agent to submit a request for you, and we will not discriminate against you for exercising your rights.
privacy.s8P1: We are not a healthcare provider, health plan, or healthcare clearinghouse, so we are not a "covered entity" under HIPAA, and information you enter on this Website is not protected by HIPAA. Instead, it is protected by this Privacy Policy, by the design choices described above (health-related information stays on your device or is collected without identifiers), and by consumer-protection laws including the FTC Act and the FTC Health Breach Notification Rule. Your medical records at your hospital or clinic remain protected by HIPAA at those organizations; connecting your health record here is your choice and copies only your active medication list into your own browser.
privacy.s9P1: The Website may contain links to third-party websites and services that are not owned or controlled by us. We are not responsible for the privacy practices of these third parties. We encourage you to review the privacy policies of any third-party sites you visit.
privacy.s10P1: The Website is not intended for children under the age of 13. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children under 13. If you are a parent or guardian and believe your child has provided us with personal information, please contact us so that we can delete such information.
privacy.s11P1: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. We will notify you of any changes by posting the new Privacy Policy on this page and updating the "Last Updated" date. You are advised to review this Privacy Policy periodically for any changes.
privacy.s12Pre: If you have any questions about this Privacy Policy or our privacy practices, please contact us at
privacy.asideText: If you have any questions about our privacy practices or how we handle your data, we're here to help.

a11y.title: Accessibility & Section 504 Compliance
a11y.s504NoticeStrong: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973
a11y.s504NoticeLink: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Final Rule
a11y.s504NoticePost: , TransplantNav LLC does not discriminate on the basis of disability in its programs, services, or activities. This website is designed to be accessible to all users, including people with disabilities.
a11y.s504P1Pre: We are committed to ensuring that our digital content meets the accessibility standards required by the HHS Section 504 final rule, which mandates conformance with the
a11y.s504WcagLink: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Level AA
a11y.s504P1Post: for patient-facing web content by May 11, 2026.
a11y.commitP1: TransplantNav LLC is committed to making the Transplant Medication Navigator website accessible to everyone, including people with disabilities. We believe that all transplant patients deserve equal access to medication assistance information, regardless of ability.
a11y.commitP2: We work to follow the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Level AA. These guidelines help make web content more usable for people with disabilities and better for everyone.
a11y.confBadgeRest: with WCAG 2.1 Level AA. "Partially conformant" means that some parts of the content do not fully meet the standard. We continuously work to maintain and improve accessibility across all features.
a11y.confP1: We performed a self-assessment of this website on February 12, 2026 and found that it meets the majority of WCAG 2.1 Level AA success criteria. As of July 6, 2026, we also run automated accessibility tests (axe-core, WCAG 2.1 A and AA rules) on our key pages before each release. No serious or critical problems are currently detected. Automated tests cannot catch every barrier. We continue manual reviews and welcome your reports. A summary of our findings is listed below.
a11y.featIntro: This website includes the following accessibility features:
a11y.featKeyboardText: All interactive elements can be reached and used with a keyboard. Use <kbd>Tab</kbd> to move between items, <kbd>Enter</kbd> or <kbd>Space</kbd> to activate buttons, and <kbd>Escape</kbd> to close dialogs and tooltips.
a11y.featScreenReaderText: We use semantic HTML and ARIA attributes so screen readers can understand the page structure. We test with JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, and TalkBack.
a11y.featRouteText: When you navigate to a new page, the page title is announced to screen readers through a live region. This ensures that screen reader users know when the page has changed.
a11y.featSkipText: A "Skip to main content" link appears at the top of each page when you press <kbd>Tab</kbd>. This lets keyboard users skip past the navigation menu.
a11y.featContrastText: Text colors meet WCAG AA contrast requirements (at least 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text). We use a custom high-contrast color palette designed for transplant patients who may have vision changes from medications. Color is never the only way to convey information.
a11y.featFocusText: All interactive elements show a visible green outline when focused via keyboard. On dark backgrounds, the outline changes to white for better visibility.
a11y.featMotionText: We respect the "prefers-reduced-motion" system setting. If you have this enabled in your operating system, animations and transitions are turned off.
a11y.featHcText: We support the "prefers-contrast" media query and Windows High Contrast Mode (forced colors). Focus indicators, buttons, and form inputs adapt to your system theme.
a11y.featSpacingText: Content remains readable and functional when text spacing is adjusted by the user. Line height, letter spacing, word spacing, and paragraph spacing can be increased without loss of content or functionality (WCAG 1.4.12).
a11y.featFormsText: All form fields have visible labels. Required fields are clearly marked. Error messages are announced to screen readers.
a11y.featTouchText: Buttons and links have a minimum size of 44 by 44 pixels, meeting WCAG 2.1 requirements. Most are 48 pixels for easier tapping on mobile devices.
a11y.featPlainText: Content is written at a 5th to 7th grade reading level. Medical terms are explained in plain language using inline tooltips with definitions.
a11y.featSimpleText: A "Simple View" toggle in the navigation increases font sizes, raises contrast, hides decorative elements, and simplifies the layout for users who prefer a cleaner interface.
a11y.featReadText: A "Read Aloud" button is available on key pages, using your browser's text-to-speech feature to read content out loud.
a11y.techIntro: This website is built with React and uses semantic HTML5 elements. Accessibility features include:
a11y.techItem3: Focus management and focus trapping in modal dialogs
a11y.techItem4: Page title announcements on route changes via aria-live region
a11y.techItem5: Minimum 16px base font size, scalable with browser zoom up to 200%
a11y.techItem6: Text spacing support per WCAG 1.4.12 (content adapts to user-modified spacing)
a11y.techItem7: Responsive single-column reflow at narrow viewports (WCAG 1.4.10)
a11y.techItem8: Print stylesheet optimized for medication and program information
a11y.compatIntro: This website is designed to work with:
compatItems: Screen readers: JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver (macOS/iOS), TalkBack (Android)
a11y.compatP2: We recommend using the latest version of Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge for the best experience.
a11y.limitsIntro: We know about the following accessibility issues and are working to fix them:
a11y.limitsInfographicText: Two informational graphics contain text that is not available as HTML. We plan to add long text descriptions.
a11y.limitsThirdPartyText: Some embedded content from external services may not fully meet accessibility standards.
a11y.limitsChatText: The chat feature uses streaming text responses that may not be announced in real time by all screen readers.
a11y.limitsP2: If any of these issues affect your use of the site, please contact us and we will provide the information in an alternative format.
testItems: Self-evaluation against WCAG 2.1 Level AA success criteria
Automated testing with Lighthouse and axe-core
Screen reader testing with NVDA and VoiceOver
Color contrast analysis of all text and interactive elements
Reading level checks using Flesch-Kincaid tools
Feedback from users, including people with disabilities
a11y.testP2: Our most recent assessment was completed on February 12, 2026.
a11y.grievIntro: TransplantNav LLC has adopted an internal grievance procedure for the prompt and equitable resolution of complaints alleging any action prohibited by Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973.
a11y.grievFileText: Any person who believes they have been subjected to discrimination on the basis of disability may file a grievance. Grievances must be submitted in writing within 60 days of the date the person filing the grievance becomes aware of the alleged discriminatory action.
grievResSteps: A written acknowledgment of your grievance will be sent within 5 business days.
An investigation will be conducted, which may include interviews and review of relevant documents.
A written determination will be issued within 30 days of receiving the grievance.
If you are not satisfied with the resolution, you may appeal within 15 days of receiving the determination.
a11y.grievAltText: This grievance procedure is available in alternative formats upon request. If you need this information in a different format due to a disability, please contact us and we will provide it promptly.
a11y.fbIntro: We welcome your feedback on the accessibility of this website. If you find a barrier or have a suggestion, please contact us:
a11y.fbHelpIntro: When reporting an issue, it helps if you include:
fbHelpItems: The web page address (URL) where the issue happened
What you were trying to do
The assistive technology you use (if any)
a11y.fbResponse: We aim to respond to accessibility feedback within 5 business days and will work to fix valid issues promptly.
a11y.extP1Pre: If you are not satisfied with our response to your accessibility feedback or Section 504 grievance, you may file a complaint with the
a11y.extP1Link: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
a11y.extP1Post: . Complaints must generally be filed within 180 days of the alleged discriminatory act.
a11y.asideText: If you need help accessing any content on this website or have accessibility concerns, we are here to help.
